jail.title: Addressing Your Jail Concern
keyPoints: First offense: Jail is rarely imposed with proper representation
Second offense: 30-day mandatory minimum (often served as home monitoring)
Third+ offense: 180-day mandatory minimum — defense strategy is critical
Early action consistently produces better jail outcomes

license.title: Protecting Your License
keyPoints: 7-day window to challenge revocation — do not miss this
Work permits and limited licenses available during revocation
Ignition interlock program can restore full driving privileges sooner
Revocation periods: 90 days (1st), 1 year (2nd), 2+ years (3rd+)

interlock.title: Understanding Ignition Interlock
keyPoints: A breathalyzer device installed in your vehicle
Required for 2nd offense+, test refusal, and high BAC cases
Cost: approximately $60–$90/month to lease
Often worth it to restore full driving privileges sooner

cost.title: Understanding the Real Costs
keyPoints: Total DWI cost without help: $10,000–$20,000+ over 3–5 years
Fines ($1,000–$3,000), insurance increases ($3,000–$8,000+), reinstatement fees
Attorney fees: $1,500–$5,000 (misdemeanor), $5,000–$15,000 (felony)
Good representation typically saves far more than it costs — free consultation available

job.title: Protecting Your Employment
keyPoints: CDL holders: DWI conviction can cause immediate CDL disqualification
Professional licenses (healthcare, law, etc.) may have reporting requirements
Acting before conviction gives the most employment protection options
Expungement may be available after a waiting period

court.title: Going to Court — What to Expect
keyPoints: First court date (arraignment): enter not guilty plea — no decisions required
Pre-trial process: evidence review, motions, and negotiation
Most cases resolve through negotiated pleas before trial
Typical timeline: 3–9 months for misdemeanor, longer for felony

actions: File petition for implied consent hearing (if license was revoked)
Write down all details about the stop and arrest
Contact a DWI attorney for emergency case review
Save and organize all paperwork (citation, DL-7, bond papers)
Do NOT drive until you confirm your license status
Work with attorney on defense strategy
Monitor blood test results if applicable
Attend implied consent hearing if requested
Explore limited license and work permit options
Plea negotiations or trial preparation begins
Review all evidence with your attorney

mistakesToAvoid.title: Waiting on the License Deadline
mistakesToAvoid.desc: The 7-day window to challenge license revocation is the most commonly missed opportunity. Even if pleading guilty to the DWI, you may be able to challenge the license revocation separately.

mistakesToAvoid.title: Talking About Your Case
mistakesToAvoid.desc: Do not discuss your case with anyone except your attorney — including friends, family, social media, and text messages. Anything you say can be used against you.

mistakesToAvoid.title: Treating Test Refusal Like a Regular DWI
mistakesToAvoid.desc: Refusal is a separate criminal charge with its own consequences and defense strategy. Many people don't realize they face two separate legal issues.

mistakesToAvoid.title: Waiting for Blood Results to Act
mistakesToAvoid.desc: Blood results take 2–6 weeks. The license challenge deadline won't wait. Don't delay taking action just because you're waiting for lab results.

mistakesToAvoid.title: Missing Your Court Date
mistakesToAvoid.desc: Failure to appear results in an automatic arrest warrant and additional criminal charges. Put your court date somewhere you absolutely will not miss it.

mistakesToAvoid.title: Underestimating Multiple Priors
mistakesToAvoid.desc: With multiple prior DWIs, mandatory minimums apply that judges have limited ability to reduce. Just pleading guilty is rarely the right strategy.

mistakesToAvoid.title: Driving Without Confirming License Status
mistakesToAvoid.desc: Driving on a revoked license adds additional criminal charges and can result in vehicle forfeiture. Always verify your status before driving.
